ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general oolong tea beverage class (FOODON_03301103); no specific entry for honey-sweetened oolong tea exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJX covers oolong tea as a category. No specific code for honey-sweetened oolong tea drink exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227313005 refers to oolong tea as a substance; the honey-sweetened beverage form is not separately coded. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: served chilled or at room temperature

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium-sized cup of approximately 500 ml, as typically served at KOI Thé and similar Taiwanese bubble tea chains in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, oolong, brewed, prepared with tap water; and USDA FoodData Central honey entry (FDC ID 169640)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for wildflower honey oolong tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea (FDC ID 173228: ~1 kcal/100g, negligible macros) with a proportional contribution of wildflower honey (USDA FDC ID 169640: ~304 kcal/100g, ~82g carbohydrate/100g) at an estimated 5–7% honey dilution in the final beverage. Medium serving (500 ml) at approximately 5% honey by weight yields approximately 15g honey per 500g serving, contributing roughly 45 kcal and 12g sugar per serving. Per-100g values reflect this blended estimate. Nutrient values for oolong tea base confirmed via USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173228.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 (Atkinson et al.) and University of Sydney GI Database — honey GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for wildflower honey oolong tea as a beverage. GI estimated from two approved components: (1) brewed oolong tea has negligible glycemic impact (effectively GI ~0 for the tea base alone); (2) wildflower honey has a published GI of approximately 55 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The blended beverage GI is dominated by the honey component. GI of 55 assigned as analogous estimate based on honey GI. Glycemic load per 500 ml serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 12g) / 100 = 6.6.

hypertension.guidance: With approximately 2 mg sodium per 100 ml and no added salt, this beverage poses negligible sodium load and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though total daily sugar intake should still be monitored.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added honey contributing free sugars and low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The wildflower honey contributes approximately 12 g of free sugars per medium (500 ml) serving with an estimated GI of 55 (low); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should account for this sugar load within their daily carbohydrate budget and consider requesting reduced-honey or unsweetened versions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible fat and sodium; polyphenol content from oolong tea
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage contains no fat, no cholesterol, and very low sodium; oolong tea polyphenols may offer modest cardioprotective benefits, though the added honey contributes free sugars that should be moderated in a heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea (Medium) is a sweetened Taiwanese-style tea beverage sold at KOI Thé outlets in Singapore. It is prepared by brewing oolong tea leaves — a partially oxidised tea originating from Taiwan and Fujian, China — and sweetening the brew with wildflower honey, which imparts a floral, mildly sweet flavour distinct from refined sugar. The medium serving is approximately 500 ml. The beverage is served chilled or at room temperature. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie drink with approximately 9 kcal per 100 ml, derived almost entirely from the honey's natural sugars (approximately 2.4 g carbohydrate per 100 ml, predominantly fructose and glucose). Protein and fat content are negligible and naturally zero, as this is a tea-based beverage with no dairy or protein additives. Sodium content is very low at approximately 2 mg per 100 ml. Oolong tea contains polyphenols including theaflavins and catechins, which have been associated with antioxidant and metabolic benefits in peer-reviewed literature. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the honey component, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 6.6 per medium serving. Clinically, the beverage is low concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to its negligible sodium and fat content, but individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to the free sugar contribution from honey. The drink is vegetarian, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but not vegan due to the use of honey.

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for KOI Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods, or ICMR-NIN. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy brewed oolong tea data with proportional honey contribution at an estimated 5% honey dilution. GI is an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. protein_g is naturally zero as this is a tea-based beverage containing no protein-bearing ingredients. 2. fat_g is naturally zero as this beverage contains no fat-bearing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy oolong tea entry (FDC ID 173228). 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are 0.0 as confirmed zero for this beverage type. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as brewed tea beverages contain no measurable dietary fibre in the liquid phase. 5. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy oolong tea source entry. 6. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 7. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 8. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 9. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 10. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 11. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 13. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 14. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 15. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 16. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 17. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 18. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source. 19. glycemic_index set to 55 (low) as an analogous estimate based on honey GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; the tea base contributes negligible glycemic load. 20. The food_name prefix 'M-' in the input record denotes medium cup size; canonical name normalised to 'Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea (Medium)'. 21. Taiwanese cuisine classification retained from input; this beverage style originates from Taiwanese bubble tea culture. 22. Serving size estimated at 500 ml for a medium cup based on standard KOI Thé medium serving convention.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for glycemic_index 55, as per standard GI classification (55 or less is low GI).
Updated relevant notes, gi_source.source_match_notes, and llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect the corrected 'low' GI category.
Normalised food_name from 'M-Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea' to 'Wildflower Honey Oolong Tea (Medium)' for canonical English form.
Added Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names.
Estimated per_100g nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy oolong tea and honey entries with transparent methodology.
Justified zero values for protein_g and fat_g in notes as naturally zero.
Documented all -1 sentinel values in notes with source explanation.
Assigned GI of 55 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database honey reference.
Populated all three health context conditions including Type 2 Diabetes with specific clinical guidance.
Built comprehensive aliases list and verified consistency between aliases_by_language and flat aliases.
Selected FoodOn, FoodEx2, and SNOMED CT closest_parent codes with gap notes; no codes invented.
